quiz_questions.question_text: The epicenter of an earthquake is:

options.text: The point on the surface directly above the focus
The point underground where the earthquake starts
The deepest point of shaking
The farthest point from the earthquake

quiz_questions.explanation: The epicenter is the location on Earth's surface directly above the focus (hypocenter), where the earthquake originates underground, and it is typically where the strongest shaking is felt.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which seismic waves are the fastest?

options.text: S-waves
Love waves
P-waves
Surface waves

quiz_questions.explanation: P-waves (primary waves) are the fastest seismic waves, traveling through rock at about 6 to 8 km per second, and they arrive at seismograph stations before all other wave types.

quiz_questions.question_text: Surface waves are the most destructive because they:

options.text: Travel the fastest through rock
Move with large amplitudes along Earth's surface where buildings and infrastructure are located
Only occur in oceans
Penetrate deepest into Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Surface waves have the largest amplitudes and travel along the ground surface where structures are built, causing the most damage through rolling and shaking motions to buildings and infrastructure.

quiz_questions.question_text: On the Richter scale, an increase of one whole number represents about _____ times more energy released.
quiz_questions.explanation: Each whole number increase on the Richter scale corresponds to approximately 32 times more energy released and 10 times greater ground motion amplitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: The point underground where an earthquake originates is called the:

options.text: Epicenter
Focus (hypocenter)
Fault line
Seismic zone

quiz_questions.explanation: The focus (also called the hypocenter) is the point within Earth where the earthquake rupture begins, and it can be shallow (less than 70 km) or deep (up to 700 km).

quiz_questions.question_text: Most earthquakes occur:

options.text: Along plate boundaries, especially the Ring of Fire
Only under oceans
Randomly across the globe
At the center of tectonic plates

quiz_questions.explanation: About 90% of earthquakes occur along plate boundaries, with the Ring of Fire around the Pacific Ocean accounting for roughly 75% of the world's seismic activity due to its many subduction zones.

quiz_questions.question_text: The difference in arrival times of P-waves and S-waves helps determine:

options.text: Earthquake magnitude only
The distance from the seismograph station to the epicenter
The type of fault that caused the earthquake
The depth of the focus

quiz_questions.explanation: Because P-waves travel faster than S-waves, the time gap between their arrivals increases with distance, and data from at least three stations can be used to triangulate the epicenter location.

quiz_questions.question_text: S-waves help identify Earth's liquid outer core because they:

options.text: Speed up through liquids
Disappear entirely at all depths
Cannot travel through liquids, creating an S-wave shadow zone
Convert to P-waves in liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: S-waves are shear waves that can only move through solid material; when they encounter Earth's liquid outer core, they are completely blocked, creating a shadow zone that proves the core is liquid.

quiz_questions.question_text: Three seismograph stations are needed to locate an epicenter because:

options.text: Three is an arbitrary standard
Two stations only give a line
One station is not accurate enough
Triangulation requires three circles to intersect at a single point

quiz_questions.explanation: Each station defines a circle of possible epicenter locations based on distance; two circles intersect at two points, but adding a third circle narrows it to a single unique intersection point.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tsunamis are generated by:

options.text: Strong winds during storms
Large undersea earthquakes that displace the ocean floor
Only land-based earthquakes
Only small earthquakes

quiz_questions.explanation: Tsunamis are primarily generated by large undersea earthquakes at subduction zones that cause sudden vertical displacement of the ocean floor, pushing massive volumes of water into motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the focus (hypocenter) of an earthquake?

options.text: The point within Earth where the earthquake rupture begins and energy is first released
The area of maximum damage after an earthquake
The surface location directly above the earthquake origin
The instrument used to record earthquakes

quiz_questions.explanation: The focus (or hypocenter) is the actual point underground where rock first fractures and seismic energy is released, and it can be classified as shallow, intermediate, or deep based on its depth.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the epicenter of an earthquake?

options.text: The point farthest from the earthquake
The weakest point of shaking during an earthquake
The underground origin point of the earthquake
The point on Earth's surface located directly above the focus where shaking is typically strongest

quiz_questions.explanation: The epicenter is the point on Earth's surface directly above the focus, and it is typically the location where shaking intensity is greatest and initial damage reports originate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are P-waves?

options.text: Waves that only travel along Earth's surface
Shear waves that only travel through solids
Slow-moving surface waves that cause the most damage
Primary compressional waves that travel fastest and can move through solids, liquids, and gases

quiz_questions.explanation: P-waves (primary waves) are compressional waves that push and pull material in the same direction they travel, move fastest of all seismic waves, and can pass through solids, liquids, and gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are S-waves?

options.text: The fastest seismic waves that travel through all materials
Surface waves that cause the most building damage
Sound waves generated by earthquakes in the atmosphere
Secondary shear waves that move material perpendicular to their travel direction and can only pass through solids

quiz_questions.explanation: S-waves (secondary waves) are shear waves that move material side-to-side perpendicular to the wave direction, travel slower than P-waves, and cannot propagate through liquids or gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a seismograph?

options.text: An instrument that detects and records ground vibrations from seismic waves using a suspended mass that remains stationary while the ground moves
A device that predicts when earthquakes will occur
A scale that measures the intensity of earthquake damage
A map showing the locations of past earthquakes

quiz_questions.explanation: A seismograph uses the principle of inertia: a heavy mass suspended from a frame remains still while the ground and frame shake, and the relative motion between them is recorded as a seismogram.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Richter scale?

options.text: A scale measuring the depth of an earthquake's focus
A logarithmic scale that measures earthquake magnitude based on the amplitude of seismic waves recorded on a seismograph
A scale measuring the observable damage caused by an earthquake
A scale rating the likelihood of future earthquakes

quiz_questions.explanation: The Richter scale is a logarithmic scale where each whole number increase represents 10 times greater ground motion amplitude and approximately 32 times more energy released.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Modified Mercalli Intensity Scale?

options.text: A scale from I to XII that measures the observable effects and damage of an earthquake at a specific location
A device for recording seismic waves
A method for predicting future earthquakes
A scale measuring the energy released by an earthquake

quiz_questions.explanation: The Modified Mercalli Intensity Scale rates earthquake effects from I (not felt) to XII (total destruction) based on observed damage, human perception, and structural impact at a given location.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'triangulation' mean in earthquake science?

options.text: Using three different scales to measure magnitude
Using data from at least three seismograph stations to pinpoint an earthquake's epicenter by finding where three distance circles intersect
Dividing the earthquake area into three zones
Measuring the three sides of a fault

quiz_questions.explanation: Triangulation uses P-wave and S-wave arrival time differences at three or more stations to calculate the distance to the epicenter, then the intersection of circles drawn at those distances locates it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is liquefaction?

options.text: The evaporation of groundwater during an earthquake
A phenomenon during earthquakes where water-saturated sediment temporarily loses strength and behaves like liquid, causing structures to sink or tilt
The flooding that occurs after a tsunami
The melting of rocks during a volcanic eruption

quiz_questions.explanation: Liquefaction occurs when earthquake shaking causes water-saturated, loose sediment to lose its strength and temporarily behave as a liquid, causing buildings to sink, tilt, or collapse.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'fault' mean in geology?

options.text: A fracture in Earth's crust along which rocks have moved relative to each other
A gap between tectonic plates where magma rises
A type of volcanic eruption
A weakness in a building's foundation

quiz_questions.explanation: A fault is a fracture or zone of fractures in rock along which the two sides have been displaced relative to each other, and movement along faults is the primary cause of earthquakes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A seismologist at Station A records P-wave arrival 30 seconds before S-wave arrival. At Station B, the gap is 60 seconds. She knows that a larger time gap means greater distance from the epicenter. Which station is closer to the epicenter?

options.text: It is impossible to determine distance from wave arrival times
Station B, because it recorded a larger time gap
Station A, because the smaller P-S time gap indicates it is closer to the earthquake source
Both stations are equidistant from the epicenter

quiz_questions.explanation: Since P-waves travel faster than S-waves, the time gap between their arrivals increases with distance; Station A's smaller 30-second gap means it is closer to the epicenter than Station B.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city engineer in a coastal area is reviewing building codes after a recent earthquake caused extensive damage to buildings on landfill but minimal damage to buildings on bedrock. She asks a geologist to explain the difference. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Buildings on landfill were older and more poorly built
Landfill is loose, water-saturated sediment prone to liquefaction and amplified shaking, while bedrock provides a stable foundation
The earthquake only struck the landfill area
Bedrock absorbs all earthquake energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Loose, water-saturated landfill amplifies seismic waves and is prone to liquefaction, where the ground temporarily behaves like liquid, while solid bedrock transmits waves without amplification and provides a stable foundation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A news reporter covering a magnitude 7.0 earthquake asks a seismologist how much more powerful it is than a magnitude 5.0 earthquake. The seismologist explains the logarithmic nature of the scale. What is the approximate energy difference?

options.text: 20 times more energy
Approximately 1,000 times more energy (32 x 32)
100 times more energy
2 times more energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Each magnitude step represents about 32 times more energy, so a two-step difference (5.0 to 7.0) represents approximately 32 times 32, or about 1,000 times more energy released.

quiz_questions.question_text: A disaster preparedness team is developing evacuation plans for a coastal city in a subduction zone. They know a large earthquake could trigger a tsunami. How much warning time might they have between the earthquake and tsunami arrival at the shore?

options.text: Several days, because tsunamis travel very slowly
Only seconds, because tsunamis travel at the speed of light
There is no warning possible for tsunamis
Minutes to hours depending on the distance from the earthquake source, because tsunamis travel at several hundred km/h across the open ocean

quiz_questions.explanation: Tsunamis travel across the open ocean at 500 to 800 km/h, giving coastal areas minutes (for nearby earthquakes) to hours (for distant earthquakes) of warning time if detection systems are in place.

quiz_questions.question_text: A structural engineer designing a new hospital in an earthquake-prone region must decide between two building sites. Site A is on solid granite bedrock, and Site B is on thick, loose sandy soil near a river. Which site is safer for earthquake-resistant construction, and why?

options.text: Site B, because it is closer to water for emergency supplies
Site B, because sand absorbs earthquake waves better
Both sites are equally safe
Site A, because solid bedrock does not amplify seismic waves or undergo liquefaction like loose, wet sand can

quiz_questions.explanation: Solid bedrock provides a stable foundation that transmits seismic waves without amplification, while loose sandy soil near water can amplify shaking and undergo liquefaction during earthquakes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student in Tokyo feels gentle swaying in her high-rise apartment and moments later sees on television that a strong earthquake struck 500 km away. She wonders why she felt shaking so far from the epicenter. Her teacher explains that certain seismic waves can travel long distances. Which waves did she likely feel?

options.text: Only P-waves, because they are the only waves that travel long distances
Sound waves from the earthquake
Aftershock waves from a different earthquake
Surface waves (Love and Rayleigh waves), which travel along Earth's surface and can cause swaying in tall buildings hundreds of kilometers from the epicenter

quiz_questions.explanation: Surface waves, particularly long-period Love and Rayleigh waves, travel along Earth's surface and can cause noticeable swaying in tall buildings at great distances from the epicenter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geophysicist analyzing seismograms from a major earthquake notices that seismograph stations between 104 and 140 degrees from the epicenter recorded no direct S-waves. She uses this observation to draw conclusions about Earth's interior. What does this S-wave shadow zone reveal?

options.text: The seismograph instruments in that zone malfunctioned
Earth's crust is thicker in those regions
The earthquake was too weak to send S-waves that far
Earth has a liquid outer core that blocks S-waves, since shear waves cannot travel through liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: The S-wave shadow zone between 104 and 140 degrees exists because S-waves cannot travel through liquid, so their absence at those angles proves the existence of Earth's liquid outer core.

quiz_questions.question_text: A community in Chile experienced a devastating earthquake and tsunami in 2010. Afterward, residents noticed that sections of coastline had risen by over a meter while other sections had subsided. A geologist explains this is related to the subduction zone. What caused these elevation changes?

options.text: The sudden slip on the subduction zone fault caused the overriding plate to snap upward in some areas and drop in others, permanently changing coastal elevation
Sea level dropped after the earthquake
Underground volcanic activity inflated the ground
The tsunami deposited enough sand to raise the coastline

quiz_questions.explanation: During a subduction zone earthquake, the overriding plate, which had been dragged down by the subducting plate, snaps upward when the fault ruptures, causing uplift in some coastal areas and subsidence in others.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school science teacher conducts an earthquake drill and afterward discusses why buildings in earthquake zones use flexible steel frames rather than rigid concrete alone. A student asks why flexibility helps. What is the correct engineering explanation?

options.text: Flexible steel frames can absorb and dissipate seismic energy by swaying without breaking, while rigid structures may crack and collapse under the same forces
Steel is magnetic and repels seismic waves
Flexible buildings are lighter and float during liquefaction
Flexible buildings are cheaper to construct

quiz_questions.explanation: Earthquake-resistant design uses flexible steel frames that can sway and deform under seismic forces without fracturing, absorbing energy, while brittle rigid structures can crack catastrophically under the same stresses.

quiz_questions.question_text: A research team installs seismometers on the ocean floor to study undersea earthquakes in real time. They aim to improve tsunami warning times. A funding agency asks how this ocean-bottom monitoring differs from relying on land-based stations alone. What is the key advantage?

options.text: Ocean-bottom seismometers are cheaper to maintain
Ocean-bottom sensors can predict earthquakes before they happen
Land-based stations cannot detect underwater earthquakes at all
Ocean-bottom sensors detect seafloor earthquakes faster and more accurately than distant land stations, providing earlier tsunami warnings for coastal communities

quiz_questions.explanation: Ocean-bottom seismometers detect submarine earthquakes earlier and with greater precision than distant land stations, reducing the time needed to assess tsunami risk and issue warnings to coastal populations.
